attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with gradual abdominal distension over six months. She reports feeling increasingly full after small meals. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as a teacher and has not travelled recently.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her abdomen is distended with a fluid wave present. There is no tenderness or organomegaly.

A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis reveals extensive low-density material throughout the peritoneal cavity with scalloping of the liver surface. The appendix appears thickened.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP), characterised by progressive abdominal distension and CT findings of low-density material throughout the peritoneal cavity with scalloping of visceral surfaces. Pseudomyxoma peritonei typically originates from a mucinous neoplasm of the appendix, which is consistent with the thickened appendix noted on imaging.

Carcinoid tumour of the appendix can occur but typically presents with features of carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhoea, wheezing) if metastatic. The CT appearance would be different, showing solid masses rather than mucin.

Tuberculous peritonitis typically presents more acutely with fever, night sweats, and weight loss. CT would show ascites with peritoneal enhancement and lymphadenopathy rather than low-density material.

Ovarian carcinoma can present with ascites and peritoneal spread but typically shows solid masses on CT and often has elevated CA-125. The pattern of spread and CT appearance would be different from the mucinous appearance seen here.

Peritoneal mesothelioma typically occurs in patients with asbestos exposure and presents with solid peritoneal masses rather than mucinous material. The CT appearance would show nodular peritoneal thickening rather than low-density material.

PMP has a characteristic 'jelly belly' appearance due to progressive accumulation of mucinous material within the peritoneal cavity. Treatment typically involves cytoreductive surgery with heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) at specialist centres.
learningPoints: Pseudomyxoma peritonei classically presents with increasing abdominal girth and 'jelly belly' appearance on imaging.
The appendix is the most common primary site for pseudomyxoma peritonei.
CT scanning is the preferred initial imaging modality for suspected pseudomyxoma peritonei.
references: Buell-Gutbrod R, Gwin K. Pathologic Diagnosis, Origin, and Natural History of Pseudomyxoma Peritonei. Am Soc Clin Oncol Educ Book. 2013 May;(33):221–5.
